subUnits.title: 1.1 Types, Properties and Preparation of Alkanols
notes: Alkanols (Alcohols) are organic compounds containing the hydroxyl (-OH) functional group attached to an alkyl group.
General Formula: CₙH₂ₙ₊₁OH or ROH where R is an alkyl group
Nomenclature: Named by replacing the '-e' ending of the parent alkane with '-ol'
1. Primary (1°) alkanols: -OH group attached to a carbon bonded to one other carbon
2. Secondary (2°) alkanols: -OH group attached to a carbon bonded to two other carbons
3. Tertiary (3°) alkanols: -OH group attached to a carbon bonded to three other carbons
• Boiling points higher than corresponding alkanes due to hydrogen bonding
• Soluble in water (lower members) due to hydrogen bonding with water
• Solubility decreases as carbon chain length increases

quiz.q: The functional group present in alkanols is:
quiz.explanation: Alkanols contain the hydroxyl (-OH) functional group attached to an alkyl group.

quiz.q: The general formula for alkanols is:
quiz.explanation: Alkanols have the general formula CₙH₂₊₁OH where n is the number of carbon atoms.

quiz.q: Ethanol is classified as a:
quiz.explanation: Ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH) is a primary alkanol because the -OH group is attached to a carbon bonded to only one other carbon.

quiz.q: The boiling points of alkanols are higher than corresponding alkanes because of:
quiz.explanation: Hydrogen bonding between -OH groups requires more energy to break, resulting in higher boiling points.

quiz.q: Fermentation of glucose to produce ethanol requires:
quiz.explanation: Fermentation requires yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) and anaerobic conditions (absence of oxygen) at 25-30°C.

quiz.q: The equation for fermentation of glucose is:
quiz.explanation: Glucose ferments to produce 2 molecules of ethanol and 2 molecules of carbon dioxide.

quiz.q: Industrial preparation of ethanol from ethene uses which catalyst?
quiz.explanation: Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) is used as a catalyst in the hydration of ethene to produce ethanol.

quiz.q: Which alkanol has the highest boiling point?
quiz.explanation: Boiling point increases with molecular size due to increased van der Waals forces. Butanol (C₄) has the highest boiling point among the options.

quiz.q: The solubility of alkanols in water decreases with:
quiz.explanation: As the carbon chain length increases, the non-polar hydrocarbon portion dominates, reducing solubility in polar water.

quiz.q: Propan-2-ol is an example of:
quiz.explanation: Propan-2-ol (CH₃CHOHCH₃) is a secondary alkanol because the -OH group is attached to a carbon bonded to two other carbons.

subUnits.title: 1.2 Chemical Properties, Etherification and Uses of Alkanols
notes: Alkanols burn in air with a clean blue flame to produce carbon dioxide and water
- Ethanol is used as a fuel (gasohol)
Primary alkanols oxidize to aldehydes, then to carboxylic acids
Oxidizing agents: Acidified KMnO₄ or K₂Cr₂O₇
Alkanols react with sodium metal to produce alkoxides and hydrogen gas
- Less vigorous than reaction of sodium with water
Alkanols react with carboxylic acids to form esters (sweet-smelling compounds)
Alkanols lose water to form alkenes
• Two molecules of alkanol combine to form an ether
Conditions: Excess ethanol, concentrated H₂SO₄ at 140°C
• Solvent for perfumes, varnishes, and medicines
• Raw material for production of ethanal, ethanoic acid, and esters
• Raw material for production of formaldehyde
• Raw material for production of acetone
diagramDescriptions: Combustion equation showing ethanol burning with oxygen to produce CO₂ and H₂O
Oxidation pathway: Primary alkanol → Aldehyde → Carboxylic acid with oxidizing agents shown
Reaction of ethanol with sodium metal showing effervescence and hydrogen gas evolution
Esterification setup showing reflux apparatus with ethanol, ethanoic acid, and conc. H₂SO₄
Dehydration of ethanol showing apparatus with conc. H₂SO₄ at 170°C producing ethene
Uses chart showing ethanol in beverages, fuel, solvent, and antiseptic applications

quiz.q: When ethanol burns completely in air, the products are:
quiz.explanation: Complete combustion of ethanol produces carbon dioxide and water: C₂H₅OH + 3O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O

quiz.q: Oxidation of a primary alkanol produces first:
quiz.explanation: Primary alkanols oxidize first to aldehydes, then further oxidize to carboxylic acids.

quiz.q: Which oxidizing agent can be used to oxidize ethanol?
quiz.explanation: Acidified potassium dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇) or potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) are common oxidizing agents for alkanols.

quiz.q: When ethanol reacts with sodium metal, the gas produced is:
quiz.explanation: 2C₂H₅OH + 2Na → 2C₂H₅ONa + H₂. Hydrogen gas is evolved with effervescence.

quiz.q: Esterification is the reaction between:
quiz.explanation: Esterification involves the reaction of an alkanol with a carboxylic acid to form an ester and water.

quiz.q: The catalyst used in esterification is:
quiz.explanation: Concentrated sulfuric acid acts as a catalyst and dehydrating agent in esterification reactions.

quiz.q: Dehydration of ethanol at 170°C with conc. H₂SO₄ produces:
quiz.explanation: At 170°C, ethanol undergoes dehydration to form ethene: C₂H₅OH → C₂H₄ + H₂O

quiz.q: Etherification of ethanol occurs at:
quiz.explanation: At 140°C with excess ethanol and conc. H₂SO₄, etherification occurs to form diethyl ether.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a use of ethanol?
quiz.explanation: Ethanol is used as a solvent, fuel (gasohol), and antiseptic (70%), but not as a pesticide.

quiz.q: Tertiary alkanols are resistant to oxidation because:
opts: The carbon with -OH has no hydrogen attached
quiz.explanation: Tertiary alkanols have no hydrogen atom on the carbon bearing the -OH group, making them resistant to oxidation.
